description: Configuration migration to address 'too many connections' errors. Review the resource implications and whether this addresses the root cause.
review_checklist: Quantify per-connection memory cost on PG
Identify connection pooler as the architectural answer
Audit work_mem implications under high connection counts
Check whether config change requires restart vs reload

ground_truth.description: max_connections = 12000 is architecturally wrong. Each PG backend has ~10MB baseline memory + transient query memory. 12000 connections = 120GB just for connection state. With work_mem = 256MB (line 8), a single sort/hash per connection can use 256MB → 12000 × 256MB = 3TB memory budget. The instance will OOM under any concurrent load. Real fix: deploy a connection pooler (pgbouncer transaction mode) and set max_connections = 200-500. App servers connect to pgbouncer; pgbouncer multiplexes onto few backend connections.

max_connections requires a server RESTART to take effect. pg_reload_conf() does NOT apply this change. The migration appears to succeed but the limit is unchanged. Migration must coordinate with operations team for a planned restart, or use rolling restart on standbys + failover.

work_mem = 256MB combined with high connection count is dangerous. work_mem is per-operation, per-connection; a single query can use multiple work_mem allocations (one per sort/hash node). Calculate: max_connections × concurrent_queries × work_mem_nodes_per_query × work_mem ≤ available RAM. At 12000 conn × 256MB × 2 nodes = 6TB worst case. Lower work_mem to 32-64MB, raise per-statement using SET LOCAL when needed.

shared_buffers = 32GB also requires restart. Same coordination issue as max_connections. Additionally, shared_buffers should be sized as ~25% of system RAM (not blindly increased) — for a 64GB instance, 32GB is at the upper safe limit; for less RAM it's harmful.

Architectural anti-pattern: 200 app servers × 50 threads each opening direct connections. Even with pgbouncer, this is fundamentally unsustainable. Use a per-app local connection pool (HikariCP for JVM, asyncpg pool for Python, etc) sized to ~5-10 connections per app. Combine with pgbouncer for multiplexing. Total backend connections ≤ 500 even at this scale.

Increasing max_connections also affects max_locks_per_transaction × max_connections shared memory allocation. Under high connection counts, lock tables can exhaust shared memory unexpectedly. Audit both settings together and size based on workload.
